title: The best trader today might just be the best prompt engineer — and honestly, that scares me
author: Woodpecker5987
selftext:
A few weeks ago I stumbled on an AI trading agent by Bitget called GetClaw. I tried it, thought it was cool, moved on.

Then yesterday I came across a post from a guy named Torikul who actually used it in a live trading competition. Not just for analysis  for real-time market monitoring, signal detection, executing positions. He didn't just use it as a tool. He basically let it run the strategy while he focused on... prompting it correctly.

**And that's where I started feeling a bit weird.**

Because the skill that seemed to matter most in his story wasn't chart reading, or years of experience, or even capital management. It was: how well can you talk to an AI?

I've been trading for a few years. I've spent time learning TA, understanding macro, managing risk. And now I'm sitting here wondering if all of that is slowly becoming secondary to whether or not you can write a good instruction to a bot.



I'm not saying it's bad. The results people are getting are real. But it's making me ask a question I didn't expect to be asking in 2026:

If the AI is doing the trading, what exactly is my edge anymore?

Is anyone else thinking about this? Where do you see the line between "using AI as a tool" vs "the AI is the trader and you're just the interface"?

comments.author: Intelligent-Log191
comments.body:
The edge is not the prompt. The edge is the constraints.

AI should not get full freedom on day one. I’d run it in stages: scan → explain setup → paper trade → small live size → only then automation.

That is how we’re building TradingWizard: TradingView-style charting first, then entry/stop/target/confidence before a bot gets execution rights.

replies.author: klymaxx45
replies.body: Yeah Prompt engineering is a thing of the past

comments.author: kwame1776
comments.body:
Why would you need to be a good prompt engineer to trade an AI bot? Trading doesn’t work like that. The prompt you give to the AI, is your trading edge, right? So why would that change from one prompt to the next? This really highlights the problem when people who can’t trade are trying to use AI to fill the gap. 

You should be able to describe your edge to the AI, and have it scan the charts for your setup. You should only need to modify it slightly whenever market regime shifts. 

If you’re interested, I’m working on a system that’s trader-driven. You suggest the plan and the AI helps you find the pattern, and helps keeps your trades consistent with your plan. The trader remains in the drivers seat.
